coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-activated g-C3N4 nanosheet/carbon dot/FeOCl nanocomposites: Photodegradation of dye pollutants and tetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Seifzadeh, Davod
Chand, Hushan
Krishnan, Venkata
coredata.dc:description: Novel g-C3N4 nanosheet/carbon dot/FeOCl (denoted as C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl) nanocomposites were fabricated for photodegradation of various pollutants and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) as an antibiotic under visible light. In this research, C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl photocatalysts were designed through a simple calcination procedure, and RhB, MB, CR, fuchsine, and TC-HCl were selected as model pollutants to test the elimination efficiency of the systems. The C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite exhibited high efficiency for elimination of pollutants. The outcomes demonstrated that 250 mL of RhB solution could be successfully removed in 60 min with 0.1 g of the optimum nanocomposite. Besides, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system revealed that 99% of TC-HCl was removed within 45 min by photocatalytic reaction. The radical quenching experiments demonstrated that h+, ⦁OH, and ⦁O2 − all contribute in the elimination reaction. Furthermore, the photoactivities of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) had no remarkable decline after four recycles, demonstrating that they had supreme structural durability. The prepared ternary photocatalyst pursues a Z-scheme charge movement mechanism that leads to significant photoactivity under visible light. The superior photoability was assigned to excellent visible-light absorption and fast charge segregation and transportation characteristics. The present research serves as a fascinating route to design and extend striking C3N4-based Z-scheme feature as promising environmental purification and solar fuel generation materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-C3N4 nanosheet/carbon dot/FeOCl
Heterostructured photocatalysts
Pollutant degradation
Z-scheme mechanism
Antibiotic
Tetracycline hydrochloride

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) EDX spectra of the synthesized materials.
(a) FESEM, (b) TEM, and (c) HRTEM images of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and XPS spectra of: (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Fe 2p, (e) O 1s, and (f) Cl 2p in the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system.
(a) FT-IR and (b) UV–vis DR spectra of the photocatalysts.
(a) TGA and (b) BET plots of as-constructed samples.
(a) The RhB elimination and (b) photodegradation rate constants of RhB on the photocatalysts.
(a) The TC-HCl elimination and (b) rate constants for the removal of TC-HCl on the photocatalysts.
UV–vis spectra for RhB elimination on the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) photocatalyst.
(a) PL, (b) EIS, and (c) Transient photocurrent plots for the materials.

(a) Effect of scavengers on the elimination of RhB over the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample. (b) Mott–Schottky plot for the FeOCl. (c) The Z-scheme mechanism for segregation of charges in the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl.
The elimination rate constants for RhB, MB, CR, and fuchsine on as-designed samples.
Construction schematic of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl samples.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-activated g-C3N4 nanosheet/carbon dot/FeOCl nanocomposites: Photodegradation of dye pollutants and tetracycline hydrochloride

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Sciences and Advanced Materials Research Center, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi Kamand

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Sciences and Advanced Materials Research Center, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi Kamand, Mandi 175075, Himachal Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel g-C3N4 nanosheet/carbon dot/FeOCl (denoted as C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl) nanocomposites were fabricated for photodegradation of various pollutants and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) as an antibiotic under visible light. In this research, C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl photocatalysts were designed through a simple calcination procedure, and RhB, MB, CR, fuchsine, and TC-HCl were selected as model pollutants to test the elimination efficiency of the systems. The C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite exhibited high efficiency for elimination of pollutants. The outcomes demonstrated that 250 mL of RhB solution could be successfully removed in 60 min with 0.1 g of the optimum nanocomposite. Besides, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system revealed that 99% of TC-HCl was removed within 45 min by photocatalytic reaction. The radical quenching experiments demonstrated that h+, ⦁OH, and ⦁O2 − all contribute in the elimination reaction. Furthermore, the photoactivities of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) had no remarkable decline after four recycles, demonstrating that they had supreme structural durability. The prepared ternary photocatalyst pursues a Z-scheme charge movement mechanism that leads to significant photoactivity under visible light. The superior photoability was assigned to excellent visible-light absorption and fast charge segregation and transportation characteristics. The present research serves as a fascinating route to design and extend striking C3N4-based Z-scheme feature as promising environmental purification and solar fuel generation materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, organic compounds like pesticides, herbicides, pharmaceuticals, and dyes severely contaminate the water resources [1]. Pharmaceutical residues as a novel group of contaminants in the environment generally have negative effects on ecosystem equilibrium and human health [2,3]. Amongst them, antibiotics have attracted more scientific attention because of wide utilization in veterinary and human medicine [4]. Especially tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) has been mostly discovered in various environmental matrices [5]. This compound is one of the most common antibiotics in usage and production that cannot be remarkably removed through traditional procedures, because of the stable chemical structure [6,7]. Besides, organic pollutants that widely utilized in textile, photographic, and printing industries are another class of hazardous contaminants discharged into effluent [8]. Risky effects of pollutants like carcinogenicity, developmental toxicity, chronic toxicity, neurotoxicity, and reproductive toxicity toward humans and animals demonstrated the importance of eliminating these pollutants from effluents [9,10]. For these reasons, the investigations were done about environmentally-green procedures for efficacious treatment of a wide range of contaminants centralized on heterogeneous photocatalytic processes using solar energy [11]. Unfortunately, conventional photocatalysts such as ZnO, TiO2, CeO2, and SnO2 have considerable activity only under UV light, due to their large optical gap [12–14]. The heterojunction photocatalysts constructed using combination of suitable semiconductors are more impressive materials to attain high photodegradation efficiency [15].

Recently, polymeric semiconductor of g-C3N4 (denoted as C3N4) has attracted enormous attention of many research communities. Owing to facile synthesis, cheap price, green characteristics, and high physicochemical stability, it is extensively utilized in photocatalysis under visible light, because it has a middle optical gap of 2.7 eV [16]. Despite some appealing features, photocatalytic activity of C3N4 is relatively poor, because of fast charge carriers recombination, low specific surface area, and also less visible-light absorption [17].

Hence, in this study, the construction of C3N4 nanosheet (denoted as C3N4-NS), was utilized as an impressive approach for increasing the surface area [18]. It is worthy to note that the construction of heterojunctions with carbon dots (denoted as CD) not only compensates the limitations of C3N4-NS via increasing electron/hole pairs segregation and improving visible-light utilization, but also induces synergetic effects, causing to enhance the movement of electrons between the components of the heterostructured photocatalyst [19].

Besides, coupling with appropriate semiconductors can increase its photocatalytic performance through enforcing charges separations and improving visible-light absorption. Lately, some investigations have been done on FeOCl, as a fascinating semiconductor, with a small optical gap of 1.59 eV in the field of pollutant degradation. However, the photostability of pure FeOCl is relatively low. Hence, many FeOCl-based nanocomposites such as TiO2/FeOCl [20], g-C3N4/FeOCl [21], carbon nanodot/FeOCl [22], and AgSCN/AgCl/FeOCl [23] have been prepared and improved stability and performance were observed when compared with pure FeOCl. For example, Yao et al. constructed a membrane through coating FeOCl to investigate its antifouling and photocatalytic activity. Remaining the photocatalyst and hindering it from aggregation were the superiorities of the study [24]. In another work, Liu et al. fabricated AgSCN/AgCl/FeOCl system and evaluated the photo-Fenton catalytic and photocatalytic activity of the designed system for the degradation of ibuprofen. This system accelerated the segregation of electron/hole pairs and boosted photoactivity [23]. Besides, Zhang et al. synthesized carbon nanodot/FeOCl, though a facile one-pot calcination method, which could generate abundant H2O2. The formed H2O2 captured with FeOCl and activated to generate abundant hydroxyl radical. This photocatalytic-Fenton system demonstrated supreme oxidation efficiency for p-chlorophenol [22].

Literature survey displayed that there is not any research about fabrication of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl nanocomposites and photocatalytic removal of pollutants and antibiotics. Herein, we deposited CD and FeOCl over C3N4-NS through a facile procedure. The C3N4-NS, CD, and FeOCl demonstrated well matched band alignment and their heterostructure demonstrated considerably increased photoactivity under visible light. This research work can suggest some guidance on construction of highly impressive photocatalysts.

Philips Xpert X-ray diffractometer by CuKα radiation was employed to gain the XRD patterns. A LEO 1430VP analyzer was applied to collect the EDX spectra. The surface structure was scanned by SEM-450 microscope. HRTEM and TEM were utilized for the microstructure analysis by S-twin microscope (Technai G 20 (FEI)). The UV–Visible DRS were assayed by Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer. The FT-IR spectra were monitored by a Perkin Elmer spectrometer. Surface electronic states were probed by Thermo Fisher X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The PL spectra were acquired from a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The TGA data were carried out with Linseis STA PT 1000. The surface area was obtained with BELSORP-mini II. A Cecile 9000 spectrophotometer was applied to gain UV–vis absorption spectra.

The C3N4, C3N4-NS, and C3N4-NS/CD were fabricated according to the previously reported procedures [25]. Briefly, the bulk C3N4 was synthesized by calcining melamine at 500 °C for 120 min and subsequently at 520 °C for another 120 min. For construction of the C3N4-NS, the resultant C3N4 was spread in petri dishes and was heated at 520°C for 120 min. The stable solution of CD was constructed through a mixture of citric acid and urea under microwave radiation (750 W) for 5 min. Moreover, for fabrication of C3N4-NS/CD, 0.5 mL of CD and 0.4 g of C3N4-including suspension were ultrasound in 100 mL of water for 5 min. Then, the dried sample in oven was calcined at 520 °C for 120 min

Besides, for synthesis of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10 wt%), 0.45 g C3N4-NS/CD was ultrasound in 100 mL of water. Then, 0.13 g FeCl3·6H2O in 20 mL water was drop by drop poured to the obtained suspension and stirred for 4 h, then fully dried at 70 °C. At last, after collecting the powders, calcined at 250 °C for 2 h ( Scheme 1).

The photocaytalysis tests were assessed at 25°C under visible light supplied by a LED lamp (50 W) for 250 mL aqueous solutions of fuchsine (9.20 ×10–6 M), MB (1 ×10−5 M), CR (2.87 ×10−8 M), RhB (1 ×10−5 M), and TC-HCl (6.2 ×10−8 M) through reading the absorptions at 540, 664, 496, 553, and 357 nm, respectively. The other conditions were fully explained elsewhere [25].

To assay the role of active species in the photocatalytic reaction, removal of RhB on the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system was investigated under visible light, utilizing 1 mmol/L of ammonium oxalate (AO), 2-propanol (2-PrOH), and p-benzoquinone (BQ) as quenchers for hole (h+), hydroxyl radicals ( • OH), and superoxide anion radical ( • O2 -), respectively. The procedure was same to the previous photoactivity reactions with the addition of scavenger in the existence of 250 mL of RhB.

Recycling of ternary C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite from RhB system was done to evaluate the sample's stability for practical applications. After every cycle, the nanocomposite was separated by centrifuge, washed with ethyl alcohol, and used for the next runs.

The electrochemical data were obtained in a standard three-electrode glassy cell through an EIS spectrometer by applying 10 mV over the frequency region of 1–100mHz. The Pt sheet was as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, the prepared samples coated with FTO glass as the working electrodes, and aqueous solution of Na2SO4 0.5 M utilized as electrolyte. The working electrodes were constructed by suspending the photocatalyst in water. Then, after scattering for 20 min with ultrasonic irradiation, the obtained slurry was dripped on a 1.0 × 1.5 cm FTO glass electrode. Finally, after drying the electrode, Nafion® 117 (10 μL; 0.5 wt%) solutions was deposited onto the electrode.

The crystalline phases and purity of the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, and C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) materials were assayed by XRD, as exhibited in Fig. 1a. The (002) and (100) planes of C3N4 are identified by two peaks at 27.4° and 13.3° (JCPDS No.87-1526), which are allocated to the interlayer stacking and planar packing of aromatic parts, respectively [26]. The lower intensity of the peaks in the C3N4-NS compared with the sole sample demonstrates that the thickness of C3N4-NS was declines and it was completely exfoliated [27]. But, according to the XRD pattern of the C3N4-NS/CD system, the diffraction peaks related to CD are absent, because of its very low amount. The pattern of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample determined that the peaks of FeOCl are in well agreement with the JCPDS No.24-1005 [28–30]. To determine composition of the systems, EDX analysis was done, and the data are demonstrated in Fig. 1b. The peaks of N and C are seen for the C3N4 and C3N4-NS systems. The spectrum of C3N4-NS/CD sample exhibits presence of N, C, and O elements. The O element, displayed in the spectrum, is because of the attendance of diverse functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl in CD [31]. The spectrum of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample exhibited the attendance of C, Fe, N, Cl, and O. The outcomes of EDX demonstrate that the systems were completely constructed, affirming the outcomes of XRD.

The surface structure of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample was assayed through FESEM and TEM analyses. As demonstrated in Fig. 2a, b, the CD and FeOCl nanoparticles adhered on the C3N4-NS. The HRTEM was utilized to gain more data about the structure of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample, and the results are observed in Fig. 2c. The interplanar spacing of 0.326 nm [32] was allocated to the (002) facet of C3N4-NS, while interface distances of 0.320 nm [33] and 0.230 nm [34] were related to the (002) and (021) planes of CD and FeOCl, respectively. Thus, the HRTEM image demonstrates the construction of junction amongst C3N4-NS, CD, and FeOCl.

For evaluation of the chemical states on the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite, the XPS spectra were applied. The Fig. 3a exhibited the main elements of the nanocomposite, affirming the presence of N, O, C, Cl, and Fe. Fig. 3b, c exhibited the attendance of C and N elements in the ternary sample. The peaks at 288.6 and 284.7 eV originate from the bonded carbon at sp2 N–C˭N and sp2 C–C bonds that are the main species assigned to C element in the C3N4-NS [35,36]. The peak at 285.8 eV is allocated to the C–OH bond, which arises from CD [31]. In Fig. 3c, the peaks at 399.9 and 398.8 eV were allocated to N element in tertiary N (N−(C)3) and C−N˭C, respectively [37]. Fig. 3d is the spectrum of Fe 2p, in which the peaks at 725.4 and 711.4 eV are associated with the Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 from FeOCl [29,38]. Fig. 3e exhibits the spectrum of O1s. The distinct peaks at 534.2 and 532.4 eV are assigned to the O element of C˭O and C–O–C bonds existed in CD, respectively [39]. Moreover, the peak located at 530.6 eV was assigned to the Fe-O bond [40,41]. Fig. 3f exhibited the spectrum of Cl 2p, which illustrated signals at 199.8 and 198.6 eV, belonging to Cl– [42]. Hence, these data affirmed the synthesis of ternary C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite.

The FT-IR analysis was utilized for characterization of the chemical bonds presented in the nanocomposites. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, a broad band observed at 3300 cm−1 in the spectra, which are allocated to the O─H bonds from adsorbed H2O molecules. The peaks in the area of 1200–1600 cm−1 are allocated to C─N and C˭N bonds from the C3N4 and C3N4-NS samples. The peak at 813 cm−1 is allocated to the triazine units [36]. For the C3N4-NS/CD system, the peaks allocated to the C˭O and –CH2 vibration can be observed in 1600–1770 cm−1 and 1431 cm−1, respectively [43,44]. In the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system, besides C3N4 and CD peaks, the Fe–O bond displays the absorption band at 510 cm−1 [45]. Hence, the FT-IR analyses revealed the presence of C3N4-NS, CD, and FeOCl in the designed C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl sample. Commonly, light-absorption capability is a main factor for photocatalytic performance. The UV–vis DRS of the synthesized samples are demonstrated in Fig. 4b. As seen, the pure C3N4 presents an absorption edge at almost 460 nm, which is related to the energy gap. Compared with the C3N4, the C3N4-NS demonstrates a blue shift, which proves exfoliation of the constructed photocatalyst [46]. With combining CD and FeOCl with C3N4-NS, the absorption of visible light by the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl samples has been developed. This is allocated to supreme optical absorption capability of FeOCl in the nanocomposites. This factor would be beneficial for improving the photoactivity under visible light.

Fig. 5a demonstrates the TGA plots of the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, and C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) samples under airflow. The first weight loss is seen from 25°C to 150°C, which allocated to evaporation of the adsorbed water on the surface of photocatalysts. The second weight loss happens in the range of 400–700 °C, which is allocated to the burning of C3N4 and CD in the photocatalysts [47]. As shown, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample starts to weight lose at a lower temperature than C3N4, which is maybe because of supply of O2 by CD and FeOCl needed for combustion of C3N4-NS [48,49]. Nitrogen sorption measurements of the samples were utilized to assay specific surface area (Fig. 5b). The BET isotherms demonstrated H3 hysteresis loops with type IV, which allocated to the mesoporous materials [50]. The surface area of the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, and C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) photocatalysts was found to be 14.6, 88.1, 51.2, and 24.1 m2 g−1, respectively. As seen, with deposition of CD and FeOCl particles, the surface area of C3N4-NS was diminished, which is allocated to the blocking some of the active sites of the C3N4-NS with CD and FeOCl particles [25]. This denotes that BET surface area is not an effective factor influencing on photoability.

Fig. 6a exhibits the photoability of the synthesized materials in the removal of RhB under visible-light excitation. The photolysis and dark tests affirmed that the removal of RhB is originally attributed to the photocatalysis process rather than photolysis and adsorption processes. When the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, and C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%) are applied as photocatalysts, the elimination efficacies of RhB are 32%, 38%, 49%, and 42% during 60 min, respectively. Thus, the coupling of FeOCl with C3N4-NS/CD illustrates a substantial improvement in the photocatalytic elimination reaction and it was reached to 100% over the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite. It is obvious from Fig. 6b that the rate constant of RhB removal on the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system is 675 × 10−4 min−1, which is appreciably faster than the sole C3N4 (17.0 ×10−4 min−1), C3N4-NS (42.0 ×10−4 min−1), C3N4-NS/CD (137 ×10−4 min−1), and C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%) (64.1 ×10−4 min−1) by a factor of 39.7, 16.1, 4.92, and 10.5, respectively. As the photocatalysis tests realized, loading more FeOCl nanoparticles leads to decreased activity, which could be assigned to destruction of the heterojunctions between CN-NS/CDs and FeOCl because of the aggregation of FeOCl nanoparticles. Hence, segregation of the electron/hole pairs could not occur impressively, leading decreased photoactivity. Furthermore, determination optimum composition for the components in multi-component photocatalysts is usually required, because weight percentages of the components could affect on the surface area, pore sizes, and light absorption capability, which are important parameters affecting on the photocatalytic activity.

TC-HCl as a typical antibiotic was also utilized to assay the photoactivity of as-constructed systems. The adsorption of TC-HCl reaches equilibrium during 1 h in dark condition as seen in Fig. 7a. The photolysis test without the photocatalysts demonstrates that the photolysis of TC-HCl can be neglected during the visible-light illumination. The C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl systems demonstrated excellent photodegradation than sole C3N4 for TC-HCl. The removal efficiency for TC-HCl enhances with the increase of the value of FeOCl and the elimination efficiency reached maximum in the existence of 10% of FeOCl. The rate constants over the pristine C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%), and C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) are 25.6 × 10−4, 50.1 × 10−4, 102 × 10−4, 74.2 × 10−4, and 375 × 10−4 min−1, respectively. Hence, the capability of the optimum photocatalyst in elimination of this antibiotics was almost 14.6, 7.47, 5.05, and 3.67 times premier than the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%), and C3N4-NS/CD photocatalysts, respectively (Fig. 7b).

The absorption spectra during the elimination of RhB over the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system is exhibited in Fig. 8. As seen, the absorption peak is at 553 nm, and as the illumination time enhances, the peak intensity diminishes to zero after the light irradiation for 1 h.

Commonly, PL spectra can present segregation efficiency of the charge carriers. Generally, a low PL intensity represents of a high segregation efficiency of the charges. Fig. 9a demonstrates the PL spectra of the materials with the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. As observed, the photocatalysts demonstrate similar spectra. However, the optimum ternary nanocomposite (C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%)) exhibits the most reduced PL intensity in comparison with the C3N4, indicating that integration of CD and FeOCl with C3N4 is beneficial for the segregation of charges from recombination. However, when adding FeOCl increased to 20% and 30%, the PL intensity of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (20% and 30%) systems considerably enhanced, which may be allocated to the aggregation of the FeOCl nanoparticles. This aggregation could destruct the heterojunctions between FeOCl particles and C3N4-NS/CD, which affects on the charges recombination. The EIS plots were recorded to assay the capability of charges for migration in the materials. As well known, a smaller arc radius demonstrates a more efficient migration efficiency of the charges. As seen in Fig. 9b, the radii of the systems are as follows: C3N4 > C3N4-NS> C3N4-NS/CD> C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%). Clearly, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system represents the smallest arc radius, indicating the least resistance for electron/hole pairs movement, which corresponds with the PL spectra. On the other hand, the photocurrent density can also ascertain the movement features of the generated charges, while higher photocurrent density demonstrates higher segregation efficiency of the charges. The transient photocurrent intensities of as-fabricated systems were done in light on/off cycles, and the data are exhibited in Fig. 9c. As seen, all systems produce photocurrent under visible light, demonstrating that they can produce electron/hole pairs. Comparatively, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system exhibits the highest photocurrent intensity, affirming that it has superior segregation efficiency for the charges. Consequently, it is concluded that the synergetic effect of the counterparts in the optimum sample plays a main role in boosting the photoactivity, which presented its capability for elimination of contaminants from the wastewater.

To ascertain the photocatalytic mechanism over the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite in RhB removal reaction, some quenchers were utilized during the photocatalytic process. In this research, 2-PrOH, BQ, and AO were added as quenchers for ⦁OH, ⦁O2 −, and h+, respectively. Fig. 10a represents the degradation reaction in the existence of the mentioned quenchers on the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite. As seen, the scavengers demonstrated notable deactivation effects for the degradation reaction on the ternary C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system, representing that ⦁O2 −, ⦁OH, and h+ have substantial roles in the degradation reaction. To ascertain the flat band potential of the FeOCl, the Mott-Schottky analysis was done, as presented in Fig. 10b. The positive and negative slopes for the linear parts of Mott–Schottky plots represent n-type and p-type semiconductors, respectively [51]. From Fig. 10b, positive slope was seen in the Mott–Schottky plot of the FeOCl sample, implying it is n-type semiconductor. Besides, the flat band potential amount was gained through the x-intercept of the tangent of the Mott–Schottky curve [52]. The flat band potential, consideration of the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, was 0.86 eV for the FeOCl. The obtained potential vs. Ag/AgCl was converted to the NHE scale by ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.2 [53]. Hence, the flat band potential of FeOCl was determined to be + 1.06 V with respect to NHE. The flat band level of an n-type semiconductors is located 0.1 V below its CB. So, the CB position of FeOCl will be + 0.96 eV. While, the CB of C3N4-NS (−1.25 eV) has been calculated elsewhere. Finally, the VB was obtained based on EVB = ECB + Eg; the VB values of C3N4-NS and FeOCl were calculated to be 1.55 and 2.55 eV, respectively. The energy band structure of C3N4-NS and FeOCl are illustrated in Fig. 10c. Under visible light, the electrons on the VB of C3N4-NS and FeOCl can be excited to their CB, accompanied through the creation of holes in their VB, because C3N4-NS and FeOCl are narrow band gap semiconductors. Using the results of scavenger tests, the charges movement in the interface follows a Z-scheme mechanism and the electrons in CB of FeOCl migrate to the VB of C3N4-NS through CD, because of excellent electron acceptor/donor characteristics of CD. As a consequence, h+ and e- are collated in the VB of FeOCl and CB of C3N4-NS, respectively, which causes to decrease recombination of the charges within C3N4-NS and FeOCl. Because the redox potential of H2O/⦁OH (+2.72 eV) and OH–/⦁OH (+2.69 eV) are more positive than the VB potential of FeOCl (EVB =+2.55 eV), the holes in VB of FeOCl cannot oxidize H2O and OH– to produce ⦁OH species, which needed for the degradation reaction. Therefore, the gathered holes over the VB of FeOCl react directly with the contaminants to degrade them.

To ascertain the stability, photoability of the recovered C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample was assayed for four successive runs and the data are exhibited in Fig. 11. After completion of the process, the photocatalyst was gathered by centrifugation, washed with ethyl alcohol, and then dried in oven. The outcomes exhibited that the optimum system could be impressively utilized for four runs without substantial deactivation, affirming its stability and reusability under the light excitation.

Effluents generally include different dyes. Hence, ability of photocatalysts to removal diverse dyes is a important factor in photocatalytic processes. To demonstrate the capability of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) for removal of different dyes, elimination of three more dyes under visible-light excitation was evaluated and the data for removal of RhB, MB, CR, and fuchsine are gathered in Fig. 12. Ability of the optimum sample in removal of RhB, MB, CR, and fuchsine was almost 39.7, 26.9, 15.2, and 20.9 times premier than the C3N4 and 16.1, 6.74, 8.11, and 6.51 of C3N4-NS, respectively. Furthermore, activity of the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) system in removal of RhB, MB, CR, and fuchsine was nearly 4.92, 4.10, 2.54, and 1.93 times premier than the C3N4-NS/CD and 10.5, 5.52, 2.88, and 2.40 of C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%) nanocomposite, respectively. Hence, activity of the Z-scheme C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) photocatalyst for impressively elimination of different dyes is affirmed.

We constructed novel Z-scheme C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl photocatalysts by a facile calcination method. The as-synthesized photocatalysts were utilized for the removal of organic contaminants like RhB, MB, CR, fuchsine and TC-HCl as a typical antibiotic for the first time. The data corroborated that the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample is able to completely removal RhB and TC-HCl from aqueous medium within 60 and 45 min, respectively. Moreover, the photoactivity of C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) was almost 39.7, 26.9, 15.2, and 20.9 times as high as the C3N4 in elimination of RhB, MB, CR, and fuchsine, respectively. Also, ability of the optimum system in removal of TC-HCl was almost 14.6, 7.47, 5.05, and 3.67 times premier than the C3N4, C3N4-NS, C3N4-NS/CD, and C3N4-NS/FeOCl (10%), respectively. The C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) sample had excellent stability after the elimination of RhB. The chemical scavenging tests demonstrated that ⦁OH, h+, and ⦁O2 – contributed in the elimination reaction over the ternary photocatalyst. More importantly, due to fabrication of the novel Z-scheme photocatalyst from cheap materials, the C3N4-NS/CD/FeOCl (10%) photocatalyst has fascinating potential for large-scale utilization in the environment protection.

Soheila Asadzadeh-Khaneghah: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing - original draft. Aziz Habibi-Yangjeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Davod Seifzadeh: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Hushan Chand: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Venkata Krishnan: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors appreciated the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for supporting this work.
